initial_context: Adults are participating in an online three-player chip-bargaining experiment. The game lasts 3 rounds. In each round, each player gets one proposal turn, following a fixed proposer schedule. The players hold colored chips, and each chip color has a private monetary value that can differ across players. During the game, each player knows their own private values and observes the current history of proposals, acceptances, trades, and chip holdings. On a proposal turn, the proposer offers a positive quantity of one chip color in exchange for a positive quantity of a different chip color. The two non-proposers privately accept or decline the proposed trade. If exactly one non-proposer accepts, the trade executes with that player. If both accept, one accepting player is randomly selected as the trade partner. If neither accepts, no trade occurs. A proposal can increase or decrease each player's payoff depending on the private chip values and current holdings. Participants are real online experiment subjects trying to earn money, but they may also care about fairness, reciprocity, agreement, efficiency, risk, competitiveness, or avoiding bad deals. Generate personas that cover the full support of plausible real participants in this chip-bargaining game, including generous, self-interested, efficiency-seeking, cautious, opportunistic, competitive, agreement-oriented, risk-taking, confused, retaliatory, trusting, and suspicious participants.
